feature_name: C64x+ VLIW core and instruction set
description: The device integrates a C64x+ fixed-point DSP core capable of issuing up to eight operations per cycle using a VLIW pipeline. It provides saturation arithmetic, bit manipulation, and SIMD-style operations on 16-bit lanes for high throughput. A Harvard architecture with separate instruction/data paths and prefetch reduces stalls. L1P/L1D caches and configurable L2 improve locality and latency. Deterministic real-time behavior depends on careful scheduling, memory layout, and cache planning.
dependencies: None; optional cache/L2 configuration during early init
Remarks: Follow TI calling conventions; avoid pipeline hazards; prime hot code/data paths before tight ISRs.

description: The L1P instruction cache (~32 KB) reduces fetch latency for hot code paths. It is transparent but benefits from locality-aware function layout and loop placement. Interaction with the L2 memory allows locking critical routines when configured as SRAM. Proper alignment and avoiding conflict hot-spots improves predictability. Use profiling to confirm hit rates in real workloads.
dependencies: Optional cache configuration via startup code
Remarks: Keep tight loops contiguous; consider L2 SRAM locking for critical paths; avoid self-modifying code.

description: The L1D data cache (~32 KB) accelerates access to frequently used data structures. Software should maximize spatial and temporal locality and align/pad to cache lines. DMA traffic must be coordinated to avoid coherency hazards on shared buffers. L2 configuration complements L1D to balance capacity and determinism. Monitor writeback/invalidate costs in ISR and DMA paths.
dependencies: Cache maintenance or non-cacheable regions when using DMA
Remarks: Use writeback/invalidate around DMA buffers; isolate MMIO as non-cacheable; align structures to cache lines.

description: L2 can be configured as SRAM, cache, or a mix to trade capacity for determinism. Locking code/data in L2 SRAM removes cache replacement effects for critical routines. As cache, L2 extends the hot working set at lower latency than external DDR2. Partitioning should be decided early and held stable across releases. Validate latency with stress under realistic EDMA and interrupt load.
dependencies: Startup L2 partitioning and memory attribute setup
Remarks: Reserve regions for ISRs/stacks/descriptors; document partition and enforce via linker script.

description: IDMA performs deterministic on-chip transfers between L2 and L1 under CPU control. It is suitable for short, latency-sensitive moves like staging code/data into L1. Programming is simpler than EDMA but limited to on-chip spaces. It complements EDMA for fine-grained prefetch or cache warm-up. Coordinate with cache settings to maintain coherency.
dependencies: None; consider cache effects
Remarks: Use for small bounded moves; prefer EDMA for large or peripheral-driven transfers; avoid starving the core.

description: The DDR2 controller provides JEDEC-compliant access to external SDRAM. Initialization requires programming SDCR/SDTIMx/SDRCR and performing the JEDEC power-up and mode-register sequence. Board-specific drive strength, termination, and VTP/PHY settings must be applied. Post-initialization pattern testing validates margins before enabling cache and EDMA traffic. Runtime maintenance includes refresh and occasional re-level checks per design.
evidence: SPRUF85 (DDR2 Controller: SDCR, SDTIM1/2, SDRCR); SPRS371 (EMIF overview)
dependencies: PSC enable for DDR2; stable PLL/clock; board DDR parameters
Remarks: Derive timing from the memory datasheet; verify with walking patterns; isolate test region from active DMA.

description: EDMA3 enables high-throughput, low-overhead data movement among memories and peripherals. PaRAM sets describe 1D/2D/3D transfers with linking/chaining for scatter-gather. Channels map to queues and TCs providing arbitration and QoS. Completion and error handling use IPR/ICR and CCERR registers. QDMA supports CPU-triggered copies with minimal latency.
evidence: EDMA3 UG (C645x): ER/EER/EESR, IPR/ICR, CCERR, PaRAM OPT/SRC/DST/A-B-CNT
dependencies: PSC enable for EDMA CC/TC; INTC mapping; event routing; coherent descriptor memory
Remarks: Align PaRAM and descriptors; clear completion in ICR after reading IPR; size bursts to cache lines to reduce thrash.

description: The Ethernet subsystem integrates two external SGMII MAC ports and a host/DSP port. The ALE provides MAC/VLAN tables, policing, and address learning. CPDMA moves packets via descriptor rings in CPPI RAM with TX/RX channels. MDIO manages external PHY discovery and link configuration. Extensive statistics counters aid bring-up and operations monitoring.
dependencies: PSC enable for 3PSW and CPDMA; PINMUX; MDIO clock; PHYs; EDMA/packet DMA setup
Remarks: Initialize MDIO and confirm PHY link before enabling traffic; configure ALE/VLANs; ensure descriptor rings are cache coherent.

description: Conventional PCI supports host/slave transactions to exchange data with a PCI host. Software configures the PCI config header, BARs, and command/status to expose DSP memory windows. Interrupts and EDMA cooperate for high-throughput bulk transfers. Proper reset sequencing and host negotiation are required at bring-up. Address translation and endianness must match host expectations.
dependencies: PSC enable; host reset sequencing; BAR/window setup; EDMA for bulk
Remarks: Validate BAR mappings with the host; confirm byte ordering; use EDMA for large moves and throttle to avoid PCI saturation.

description: UHPI allows a parallel host to access the DSP memory map directly. Control covers bus width, resets, and ready/hold timing for robust transactions. The interface supports polled or interrupt-driven synchronization. It simplifies host-assisted boot and diagnostics. Arbitration must avoid contention with CPU and EDMA accesses to shared regions.
dependencies: PSC enable; host timing/handshake; INTC mapping if used
Remarks: Program HPIC for width and reset; validate hold/ready timing; partition shared memory to prevent races.

description: VLYNQ provides a point-to-point serial link that memory-maps remote resources. Configuration negotiates link rate, framing, and address windows on both ends. Once up, the peer’s regions appear in the local address map for load/store and EDMA. Error/status registers support recovery from link faults. Bring-up requires consistent mapping and initial handshakes.
evidence: Device integration guide: VLYNQ chapter (link control, window mapping)
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; peer configuration; window mapping
Remarks: Start with small polled reads/writes; verify window alignment; only then enable EDMA over the link.

description: McASP supports I2S, TDM, and DIT with independent TX/RX clocks and frame syncs. Serializers are flexibly assigned to transmit or receive lanes. Data flows through XBUF/RBUF with rich error/status reporting and interrupts. GBLCTL sequences the state machines for safe start/stop. EDMA is typically used to service streams with minimal CPU intervention.
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; EDMA channels; INTC mapping
Remarks: Follow the documented start/stop sequence; verify clock polarity/frame timing; align EDMA frames to slot boundaries.

description: TSIP provides multi-link telecom interfaces with programmable frame-sync and clocking. Separate TX/RX links support time-slot based transfers for TDM networks. FIFOs and status registers enable low-latency servicing via EDMA or interrupts. Integration eases attachment to framers and baseband devices. Correct pinmux and timing are essential for reliable operation.
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; EDMA channels; INTC mapping
Remarks: Validate framing and bit rates against the framer; use EDMA to meet slot deadlines; monitor underrun/overrun flags.

description: SPI supports master/slave operation with programmable CPOL/CPHA and word length. Formats and delays (SPIFMT/SPIDELAY) tailor timing to slave devices. Transfers use SPIDAT and SPIBUF with optional interrupts for flow control. EDMA can offload long transfers for throughput. Correct chip-select polarity and pinmux are required for data integrity.
evidence: SPRUF92 (SPI: SPIGCR0/1, SPIFMTx, SPIDELAY, SPIDATx, SPIBUF)
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; chip-select wiring; optional EDMA/interrupts
Remarks: Reset and configure before enable; match CPOL/CPHA to slave; consider EDMA for long bursts and align buffers.

description: The I2C module supports standard and fast modes in master or slave roles. Prescaler and SCL low/high registers set precise bit timing. ICMDR/ICSTR control transfers and report status, NACK, and arbitration loss. External pull-ups on SDA/SCL are mandatory for signal integrity. Interrupts simplify multi-byte and error-handling flows.
evidence: SPRUF94 (I2C: ICOAR, ICSAR, ICPSC, ICCL/ICCH, ICCNT, ICMDR, ICSTR)
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; external pull-ups; INTC mapping
Remarks: Handle NACK/arbitration lost per ICSTR; program ICCNT correctly; assert STOP only after last ACK.

description: The UART is 16550-like with configurable word length, parity, and stop bits. RX/TX FIFOs reduce interrupt rate and improve latency. Baud is generated from the input clock via DLL/DLH divisors with optional prescaling. PWREMU_MGMT gates clocks for low power when idle. EDMA can service sustained high data rates if justified.
evidence: SPRUF93 (UART: THR/RBR, LCR, FCR, DLL/DLH, IER/IIR, PWREMU_MGMT)
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; reference clock; INTC mapping
Remarks: Reset FIFOs before enabling; validate baud error; consider EDMA only when throughput requires it.

description: GPIO exposes up to 32 pins configurable per-bit as inputs or outputs. Direction is set in DIR and data written via SET_DATA/CLR_DATA or read via IN_DATA. Edge-detect logic supports rising/falling edges with latched status for interrupts. Bank-level enables and INTSTAT01 manage interrupt delivery. GPIO can source EDMA sync events in certain modes.
evidence: SPRUF95 (GPIO: DIR, SET/CLR_DATA, IN_DATA, SET/CLR_RIS_TRIG, BINTEN, INTSTAT01)
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; INTC mapping; optional EDMA trigger
Remarks: Enable via PSC before access; clear edge flags; debounce externally for mechanical inputs.

description: Each timer provides a 64-bit counter that can be split into two 32-bit timers. Modes include periodic and one-shot with capture/compare and interrupt generation. Prescalers derive precise time bases from the input clock. Timers support timestamping, periodic ticks, PWM-like waveforms, and capture. Keep ISRs short to minimize jitter and latency.
evidence: Device timer chapter (TIM12/TIM34, PRD12/PRD34, TCR, TGCR, INTCTL)
dependencies: PSC enable; INTC mapping; optional PINMUX for capture/PWM
Remarks: Choose 64-bit or dual 32-bit mode early; compute prescalers accurately; defer work from ISR to tasks.

description: The PLL multiplies an external reference to generate core and system clocks. Peripheral dividers derive clocks for subsystems such as EMAC, EDMA, and timers. Reprogramming requires a defined lock/unlock and command/status sequence. Stability must be ensured before enabling DDR2 and high-speed peripherals. Changes can impact peripheral timings and require coordinated reconfiguration.
evidence: SPRS371 (PLLC: PLLCTL, PLLM, PLLDIVx, PLLCMD, PLLSTAT)
dependencies: Stable reference clock; correct programming order; PSC sequencing
Remarks: Follow the documented lock sequence and poll PLLSTAT; update dividers coherently; verify derived clocks during bring-up.

description: PSC manages power and clock gating of modules grouped into domains. Each peripheral’s LPSC must be transitioned to ENABLE before register access. PTCMD starts transitions and PTSTAT/MDSTAT provide status. Proper sequencing avoids bus stalls and reduces power at idle. Unused modules should be disabled once the system is stable.
dependencies: Required by all peripheral drivers; domain/state sequencing
Remarks: Always enable via PSC before touching registers; poll for transition completion; gate unused modules to save power.

description: Many pins are multiplexed between peripheral functions and GPIO. KICK0R/KICK1R must be written with keys to unlock protected system configuration including PINMUX. Correct selection prevents signal conflicts and enables required interfaces. Reserved fields must be preserved when writing pinmux registers. Relock after configuration to protect against unintended writes.
dependencies: KICK unlock sequence; ordered with PSC to avoid unsafe toggling
Remarks: Centralize pinmux in board init; document conflicts; apply read-modify-write to preserve reserved bits.

description: The C64x+ interrupt model maps many system events onto INT4–INT15 via INTMUX. The Event Combiner aggregates up to 128 events into four outputs to simplify routing. ISRs must clear peripheral status before acknowledging the controller to avoid retrigger. EDMA completion/error events integrate cleanly into this framework. A centralized vector table and small ISRs reduce latency.
dependencies: Peripheral enables; INTMUX mapping; optional event combiner
Remarks: Keep ISR prolog/epilog minimal; clear source first; defer heavy work to tasks and use EDMA where possible.

description: The ROM Bootloader selects a boot source via strap pins and minimal configuration. Supported sources include I2C, SPI, UART, PCI, and UHPI depending on board wiring. The RBL fetches a TI boot table to initialize memory and transfer control to the application. Software typically reprograms PLL and enables required PSC modules post-boot. Capabilities can vary by ROM revision.
dependencies: Board straps; ROM revision; external boot media
Remarks: Keep first-stage minimal and robust; hand off to unified board init that programs PLL/PSC/DDR deterministically.

description: The IDLE instruction gates the core clock until a wake interrupt occurs. PSC can further gate module or domain clocks for deeper savings. Wake sources must remain enabled and routed correctly through the event framework. Entry/exit must preserve data integrity and timing commitments. Proper use reduces thermal load and improves energy efficiency.
dependencies: Wake interrupt routing; PSC domain configuration
Remarks: Use IDLE in wait loops; ensure wake interrupts are unmasked; quiesce DMA before gating domains.

description: JTAG provides halt, trace, and real-time debug capabilities for development. Some peripherals include emulation-aware controls to keep clocks active during halt. Debug can perturb timers, DMA, and links if used without care. Boot timing and attach order may influence system behavior. Follow emulation notes to avoid interfering with real-time operation.
dependencies: External emulator; toolchain integration
Remarks: Avoid halting during active transfers; enable emulation-aware options; script attach/reset sequences.

description: Peripherals expose status/error flags typically cleared by writing one to the bit. EDMA reports completion and errors via IPR/ICR and CCERR. Robust drivers check, log, and clear these promptly in ISRs or polling loops. Centralized error paths simplify diagnostics and field triage. Counters and timestamps aid in recognizing recurring faults.
dependencies: Per-driver ISR/polling; logging infrastructure
Remarks: Unify error-reporting conventions; follow W1C semantics; include counters and rate-limit logs.

description: MAR/MPAX configuration controls cacheability and address extension for external regions. Correct attributes ensure DMA buffers and MMIO windows behave as intended. These settings complement L2 partitioning for balanced performance and determinism. Misconfiguration can cause stale data or bus faults under load. Perform early and verify with cacheability tests.
dependencies: Privileged CPU configuration; coordinated with cache and EDMA usage
Remarks: Mark MMIO non-cacheable; align DMA buffers; document attribute tables and lock them down.

description: No dedicated security/crypto accelerators are described in public manuals for this device. Secure boot or hardware access control is not documented for C6452. If security features exist, they are silicon- or system-dependent and outside standard guides. Software should assume no hardware crypto or secure boot by default. Reassess on newer silicon or with NDA collateral.
dependencies: UNKNOWN
Remarks: Use software crypto if required; rely on board/system protections; review updates for any added security blocks.
